---
title: "Missing Middle: Belarusian Entrepreneurs in the Western Support System"
description: "Between sanctions policy and humanitarian support, there is a structurally absent layer that mature democracies consider the backbone of economic sovereignty. Some half a million Belarusian self-employed and micro-entrepreneurs of the real sector have no addressable category in any existing donor taxonomy."
author: "Sergey Panfilov"
date: "2026-05-25"
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
lang: en
categories: [Belarus, economy, civil society]
reading-time: true
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-title: "Contents"
---

## Introduction

Western discourse on Belarus over the past five years comes down to two lenses: the repressive regime as an object of sanctions policy, and Belarusian civil society as an object of humanitarian support. Each is analytically well developed, has its established experts, its toolkit, and its own budget line with most Western donors. Between them, what mature democracies consider the backbone of economic sovereignty is structurally absent — the layer of self-employed and micro-entrepreneurs of the real sector.

In the Belarusian case, as of autumn 2025, this layer comprises some 50,000 officially registered artisans, 218,000 active individual entrepreneurs, up to 80,000 of their hired employees, and around 120,000 owners of micro-enterprises. In total — about half a million people, or roughly 12% of the country's economically active population, not counting the households dependent on their activity.

Despite this substantial scale, the layer has no addressable category in any of the existing donor taxonomies. Programmes to support Belarusian business in exile are designed either for the technology sector or for a generic "immigrant-startup founder" based on a model historically developed for developing countries. Half a million active real-sector entrepreneurs, caught in the position of simultaneous loss of their own country's institutional environment and inability to legally transfer capital to a new jurisdiction, are simply not visible within these frames.

This invisibility has a second cause. Belarusian business in Western capitals is currently represented by people without their own entrepreneurial experience in the segment under discussion — academic economists, former officials, "reform" experts.

## Basic definitions

### Who counts as an entrepreneur here?

By "Belarusian self-employed and micro-entrepreneur," this essay means the real-sector layer formed over thirty years of coexistence with an authoritarian state, numbering, as noted above, around half a million active participants. This definition requires two clarifying boundaries.

From the Belarusian technology sector, this layer differs structurally. Statistics on businesses with Belarusian owners in Poland — where the bulk of the relocated business diaspora is concentrated — show that IT accounts for only about 6% of the volume; the remaining 94% work in trade, services, construction, logistics, crafts, small manufacturing, and food service. These businesses are materially tied to the jurisdiction — by warehouses, equipment, local clientele, licences. Unlike mobile IT teams, they cannot arbitrage tax rates or relocate in response to changed conditions; they either stay or are liquidated.

From the "developing-country" micro-business of the Global South, onto which donors habitually project their support frame, the Belarusian layer differs radically. These are not nascent entrepreneurs needing initial training in running a business and basic financial literacy, but a layer with decades of operational experience, an established client base, sector-specific expertise, and — critically — the skill of operating in an institutionally hostile environment. Their problem is not a deficit of entrepreneurial competencies, but three connected forms of loss: of their own country's institutional environment; of accumulated starting capital — for a significant share of those who relocated, it was left behind in Belarus, confiscated, lost in forced asset sales, or simply not taken out during an emergency departure; and, finally, of access to starting capital in the receiving jurisdiction, where banking discrimination on national grounds, refusals to open accounts, and the unavailability of standard lending instruments make even the most basic business restart impossible by the means that entrepreneurs from any other country rely on.

Henceforth in the essay, "Belarusian entrepreneurs" refers specifically to this layer — self-employed and real-sector micro-entrepreneurs, delimited along the two axes above. This is a deliberate narrowing of the term; it is set against the dominant notation in Western discussions, where "Belarusian business" means either IT by default or an abstract "private sector" with no structural detail.

### The missing middle

In development economics, the term "missing middle" has an established meaning. It refers to the structural deficit of medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in developing countries: the population is employed predominantly in informal micro-business and agriculture on one pole, in a few large companies on the other, while the middle layer remains weakly represented. The literature treats this gap as an obstacle to growth and grounds the corresponding donor logic — SME lending programmes, formalisation, business incubators.

Applied to the Belarusian case, the following matters. The gap lies not in the Belarusian economy itself, but in the donor taxonomy — the system of categories through which Western institutions "see" their objects of support. The layer described above fits none of the existing frames. It is not a tech startup, around which accelerators and visa programmes like Poland Business Harbour are built. It is not a nascent entrepreneur of the Global South, for whom incubators, entrepreneurship training, and grassroots consulting are designed. It is not an SME in the sense of development programmes from country development banks targeting medium-sized firms of fifty employees and above.

This missing link in donor classification is hereafter designated as the *missing middle of donor taxonomy*. This is not a local Belarusian anomaly; it is a systemic defect of the donor apparatus that the Belarusian case merely lays bare with particular clarity — because the layer in question here is quantitatively comparable to a substantial share of the economically active population, while external pressure in the form of sanctions, banking discrimination, and visa restrictions only worsens its position.

### Infantilisation of the entrepreneur

Applying the nascent-entrepreneur frame to the Belarusian layer is not a neutral methodological error. It has a stable cause that explains why the frame is reproduced despite its evident inadequacy.

The educational product — a training, a guide, an incubator — offers a number of operational advantages to the party applying it. It is easily scaled, has formal KPIs (number of participants, reach, events held), is measured by inputs rather than outcomes, requires no substantive expertise in the recipient's sector, and bears no responsibility for the macroeconomic outcome. Solving real tasks — capital relocation, countering banking discrimination on national grounds, integration in the receiving jurisdiction — has the opposite properties: it demands operational expertise, is measured by outcomes, does not reduce to a standardised product, and implies institutional accountability.

From this asymmetry of incentives arises a stable practice in which the implementer — whether a donor operator, an expert organisation, or a representative of the nominal opposition in exile — chooses to work with a symbolic object ("a notional Belarusian entrepreneur in need of training") rather than with the real one ("half a million real-sector entrepreneurs in need of relocation infrastructure"). Applying a frame designed for the novice to a mature business layer is *infantilisation of the entrepreneur*. This is not a rhetorical figure and not a moral characterisation; it is a description of a working mechanism in which the substitution of object relieves the implementer of the need for real expertise and real responsibility.

## Part I. The layer that is not described

### Origins

Belarusian real-sector entrepreneurs were formed not out of ideology and not out of choice, but out of necessity. The first wave were the late-Soviet cooperative members and shuttle traders of the late 1980s and early 1990s, for whom private initiative was a way to survive the economic collapse of a disintegrating system. The second wave came with the 1990s, with their privatisations, shortages, hyperinflation, absence of banking infrastructure, and wholesale replacement of state services by private ones; in these conditions, a substantial part of the urban population passed through a stage of self-employment by necessity, regardless of their own entrepreneurial inclinations. The third wave, already under the Lukashenka regime, was shaped by the same thing — state pressure pushing people into the private sector not as a space of opportunity, but as a survival zone where one could at least partly avoid direct dependence on a state salary, position, and apartment.

From these origins follows a characteristic that distinguishes the Belarusian layer simultaneously from the self-employed of developed democracies and from the nascent entrepreneurs of developing countries. It was not formed as a voluntary exit from corporate employment toward greater autonomy — the model typical of mature market economies — nor as the initial stage of economic development in an agrarian or early-industrial country — the model typical of the Global South. It was formed as adaptation to a specific post-Soviet trajectory in which the state first destroyed its own economic infrastructure and then built a new institutional environment that works systematically against private initiative. Over all thirty of those years, each decision taken by the Belarusian state — from periodic tightenings of the simplified tax regime to the outright closure of specific categories of individual entrepreneurship in 2022–2025 — consistently reduced the space of legal activity, forcing the layer to adapt or go underground.

### The dynamics of forced liquidation

The dynamics of individual entrepreneurs in Belarus over the past three years show that this layer is in the process of forced administrative dismantling: from approximately 270,000 at the start of 2023, the number of IPs (individual entrepreneurs) fell to 252,000 at the start of 2024, to 237,000 at the start of 2025, and to around 218,000 by autumn 2025. In the first nine months of 2025 alone, the layer lost about 20,000 participants, and the trend is continuing.

The legal basis for the dismantling was provided by the 2024 Council of Ministers resolution and the law on reorganising individual entrepreneurship[^1], which fixed a closed list of activities permitted to IPs. According to Ministry of Economy estimates, by autumn 2025 about half of the IPs not included in the permitted list had used the "seamless" transition or changed their type of activity; the other half were either in the process of liquidation or already liquidated.

The Belarusian authorities call this procedure "structural restructuring" or "seamless transition"; in reality it is an accelerated liquidation of the IP as a mass status, since transforming into a legal entity requires capital, accounting infrastructure, and a steady tax burden that are beyond the reach of the overwhelming majority of micro-entrepreneurs. Re-registration formally preserves the business's legal existence but in practice cuts off a significant part of the layer from the ability to continue operating.

### How the layer differs from medium and large business in Belarus

The layer of Belarusian entrepreneurs differs from medium and large business not only in size, but in the character of its tie to the country. Medium and large business that remained in Belarus after 2020 mostly remained deliberately — because of attachment to material assets, licences, contracts with state counterparties, or because of direct or indirect arrangements with the regime that secure business protection in exchange for loyalty. This segment is largely integrated into the state's economic infrastructure and, in sanctions terms, is of greatest interest to Western regulators as a potential channel for circumventing restrictions against the regime.

Micro-entrepreneurs remained in Belarus for the opposite reason. Relocation, for them, carries critical risks: material assets — a small workshop, shop, hair salon, café, truck, warehouse — cannot be moved; the client base is local; accumulated capital is in most cases insufficient to restart a business in a foreign jurisdiction; legal channels for moving funds out of the country are closed or significantly restricted. This is a layer that stayed not out of loyalty and not out of calculation, but out of the impossibility of leaving without losing everything.

From this difference follows the different political nature of the two segments. Medium and large business inside the country acts either as part of the regime's infrastructure or as an object of sanctions control. The Belarusian entrepreneurial layer, in the sanctions lens, is formally politically neutral: it is neither a source of support for the regime nor an object of sanctions. In practice, however, it ends up hostage to a situation in which the Belarusian state pushes it out of the legal field, while Western foreign policy, not distinguishing different segments within the Belarusian economy, in effect reinforces that pressure through banking discrimination and visa restrictions applied on national grounds. And yet, in the practical toolkit of Western support, this layer does not appear as a category of its own. The Belarusian private sector as "the last hope of sovereignty and transformation" — a formulation reached over recent years by independent academic literature[^2] — is declaratively recognised as critical, but has no operational addressee.

## Part II. The triple shock and the point of no return

### Shock one: the pandemic

When Europe was locking down in March 2020 and Western governments were launching small-business support programmes measured in hundreds of billions of euros, the Belarusian state chose demonstrative denial of the pandemic and, at the same time, the rejection of any private-sector support. State enterprises, as in previous crises, received financing, tax deferrals, and access to directive lending; the private sector was left alone with falling demand, broken logistics chains, and closed borders.

This first shock had a twofold meaning. On one hand, it confirmed what the layer already knew about itself — that the state is not its partner under any circumstances, including extraordinary ones. On the other, it demonstrated to the entrepreneur, more sharply than ever, their own autonomy: the pandemic was survived without support, on internal resources, flexibility, and the survival skills built up over thirty years in an institutionally hostile environment. Paradoxically, the Covid shock strengthened the layer as a subject, separating it — at the level of self-awareness — from the state economic infrastructure. By August 2020, a substantial part of Belarusian entrepreneurs were approaching the situation no longer as loyal taxpayers expecting fairness from the state, but as an independent social group that had learned from its own experience that nothing should be expected from the state except pressure.

### Shock two: political repression

The August 2020 presidential election and the mass protests that followed gave this self-awareness a political expression. Belarusian business — from small to medium enterprises, from artisans to factory owners — turned out to be one of the most significant groups providing resources to the protest movement. Some entrepreneurs publicly took part in marches and strikes; some financed medical aid for the injured and legal defence for the detained; some simply refused to keep paying taxes "as if nothing was happening." The regime read this signal accurately and responded accordingly.

The repression against entrepreneurs after 2020 did not take the form of general criminal prosecution; it had a clearly defined structure of administrative and economic pressure. Targeted inspections, licence revocations, backdated tax claims, pressure through landlords and counterparties, demonstrative arrests of the most visible figures — all of this shaped a regime of managed fear for the layer. Added to this was the hostage syndrome in the literal sense: entrepreneurs who had relocated to Poland or Lithuania understood that any public gesture against the regime could cost the freedom of their employees, partners, or relatives still in Belarus.

Notably, the repression did not destroy the layer — it changed its configuration. Some of the most active and visible entrepreneurs left the country in the first two years after 2020, taking — where possible — their business or part of it. Some went underground, switching to informal settlements and shadow supplies. Some remained within the legal field but in a regime of strict political neutrality, refusing any public statements and minimising visibility. The thirty-year skill of operating in a hostile environment found a new application: the layer adapted, but the cost of adaptation was significant — a de facto renunciation of political subjectivity inside the country.

### Shock three: sanctions isolation and the generational fork

February 2022 and Lukashenka's complicity in Russian aggression against Ukraine triggered the third and most structurally dangerous process for the layer. Western sanctions against the regime, politically justified, did not in their practical implementation distinguish between different segments within the Belarusian economy. The Belarusian passport became what entrepreneurs themselves describe as "toxic" or "acidic": grounds for refusal to open bank accounts in the European Union, for payment systems refusing to serve Belarusian legal entities, for Western counterparties terminating relationships, for visa restrictions, and for a general presumption of unreliability regardless of the owner's individual stance.

The substantive consequence of this isolation was the fork that the layer faced in 2022–2024. Those entrepreneurs who could relocate did so — predominantly to Poland, less so to Lithuania and Georgia; as of early 2026, the register of the Centralny Ośrodek Informacji Gospodarczej records 7,093 active companies with Belarusian capital in Poland, generating around 70% of all foreign revenue of Belarusian business outside the CIS. This part of the layer ended up in a more difficult but fundamentally workable position: the Western market, despite all the barriers of banking discrimination and visa restrictions, remains its working environment.

Within this aggregate figure, the dynamics matter. The peak of new registrations came in 2022 — 1,501 registrations, a direct response to the sanctions shock. After that the line descends: 2023 gives around 1,200 registrations, 2024 around 1,050, 2025 around 870 — a 42% reduction. The hypothesis that the relocation channel is saturated works only for the IT segment, which by 2023 had largely already moved. For the real sector, the relocation channel never opened: the numbers are falling not because those willing have run out, but because the instruments through which a real-sector micro-entrepreneur could relocate are absent. The collapse of Belarusian-rooted business openings in Poland in 2025 coincides with the deadline for the liquidation of a significant share of Belarusian IPs, set at 31 December 2025 — that is, at the very moment when, inside Belarus, possibilities for legal entrepreneurial activity are being closed en masse, in Poland the registration of Belarusian companies reaches a four-year low.

Those entrepreneurs who are forced to stay — and this, as shown above, is the overwhelming majority of the layer — found themselves in a position best described by the word "ejection." The Western customer left; Western suppliers stopped shipments; banking settlements with the European Union became technically and operationally difficult. The remaining layer was left with two alternative directions — and both lead in the same direction.

The first direction is deepened service of the Belarusian state sector. In an economy where the share of the state sector has historically exceeded half, private micro-business has always largely occupied a subcontractor niche — logistics, repairs, materials supply, IT servicing, catering at factories, small services for state-enterprise workers. After 2022, this niche expanded: with external markets closed, the state customer becomes the main or only source of income for many micro-entrepreneurs. The second direction is reorientation to the Russian market, where after 2022 demand for "parallel imports," workaround logistics schemes, services for relocating Russian assets to third jurisdictions, and related services rose sharply. Belarus became a natural transit link, and a substantial part of real-sector micro-entrepreneurs ended up embedded in this infrastructure — sometimes deliberately, more often by necessity, as the only available source of orders. At the macro level, this movement is recorded in trade statistics: by 2024, about two-thirds of all Belarusian exports went to the Russian market, which moves the reorientation described above from the plane of individual cases to the plane of the structural condition of the national economy[^5].

Both directions have not only an economic but also a cultural dimension. The class of Belarusian entrepreneurs, formed over thirty years in a regime of internal resistance to the state economy and cultural gravitation toward European markets and standards, has been, over the four years since 2022, restructured into a class for which the principal customer is the Belarusian state or the Russian market, and the Western direction is a closed or fundamentally inaccessible alternative. This structural change of the layer happens not at the level of declarations or identity, which are inertial and change slowly, but at the level of daily operational ties — who pays, with whom contracts are concluded, in what currency settlements run, which trade fairs are attended, which business communities one belongs to, through which banks payments pass. In one generation, if the current sanctions configuration and the current absence of Western support programmes for this layer persist, carriers of European values within Belarusian entrepreneurship will become a historical phenomenon rather than an active social group. This will happen without anyone's special effort — by the inertia of an already-running process.

This fork, in essence, is the central stake of the essay. The Belarusian real-sector layer does not need rescue in the humanitarian sense — it is capable of surviving, as it has demonstrated consistently in 2020, in 2022, and continues to demonstrate now. The question is not whether it survives, but in which direction it survives. The current inaction of Western donors and Western governments toward this layer is not neutral inaction; it is, in effect, a choice in favour of its fusion with the authoritarian state economy and the Russian market, since no third alternative for the layer remaining in the country exists today.

## Part III. The West's double blindness

### Donor frame and infantilisation in practice

The conceptual apparatus introduced above describes infantilisation of the entrepreneur as a functional advantage for the party applying it: the educational product is easily scaled, measured by inputs, requires no operational expertise, and bears no responsibility for the macroeconomic outcome. This functionality also defines the practical shape of donor programmes directed at the Belarusian private sector in 2020–2025.

A clarification is needed here, without which the further argument risks being read perversely. The infrastructure of manuals, guides, training, and incubators for opening a business in Poland in particular and in the European Union in general is not merely well represented — it is excessively so. Polish state agencies, regional entrepreneurship centres, industry chambers, private business consultants, national and immigrant organisations, university programmes — all of them, in one form or another, produce educational products on opening a business. Anyone wishing to obtain a basic understanding of legal forms of activity, tax regimes, support programmes, and access to financing in Poland can do so within several hours, mostly for free, in Polish, English, Ukrainian, and to a significant extent in Russian. Support for startups in the EU is represented even more densely: venture funds, business angels, accelerators, corporate programmes, state innovation-development initiatives exist in numbers many times exceeding actual demand from startup communities.

From this follow two points essential for the further discussion. First: the proposed critique of the educational product is not directed against education as such or against the existing entrepreneurship-support infrastructure. Education on the ground — in the receiving jurisdiction, by means of the receiving state, free or nearly free — is available and works. Second: replicating that same infrastructure through donor programmes on top of what already exists, under the label of "support for Belarusian business," is not added value but idling. Donors finance what already exists in the receiving country in excess, and do not finance what does not exist there — institutional solutions specific precisely to the Belarusian situation (access to IP status for Belarusians on ordinary migration statuses, compliance protocols for Belarusian legal entities, mechanisms for legalising capital being moved out from under the sanctions regime). The critique of infantilisation, therefore, is a critique of precisely this substitution: not of education in general, but of education in place of institutional work where the latter is what is missing.

A typical donor project in this area is built to the same template. The target audience is designated as "Belarusian entrepreneurs in exile" with no structural detail, embracing in a single category a recent IT employee who has opened a *jednoosobowa działalność gospodarcza* for personal tax-optimisation purposes, the owner of a construction firm with a twenty-year history in Belarus, a student startup founder, and an artisan. Support instruments are chosen from a standard set: a series of webinars on "how to open a business in Poland," basic legal consultations, a business incubator, a consulting voucher, a training guide on legal forms of activity, a conference with experts. Success metrics are formed by reach — number of participants, number of guides issued, number of events held.

If we set the real needs of the layer described in the second part against the standard donor product, the gap becomes obvious. Capital relocation, in conditions where it cannot be legally taken out of Belarus and at the same time the receiving jurisdiction requires proof of the legality of its origin, is a technical and legal task, solvable through bilateral regulation, tax amnesties, and the adaptation of existing instruments such as the Polish *Ulga na powrót*. An educational product does not solve this task, because the layer already knows that capital needs to be legalised; what it does not know is how to do so under double blocking. Banking discrimination on national grounds is a task solvable through clear compliance protocols for Belarusian legal entities with Polish or Lithuanian residency, not through explaining to the entrepreneur banking rules they already know. Access to starting capital for someone arriving without savings is a task solvable through specialised credit lines, state guarantees, or partnerships with regional development funds. The list could be extended, and in each case the structure is the same: the real task requires institutional intervention; the standard answer is an educational product.

This gap is reproduced not because of an information shortage. The structure of Belarusian business in exile has been described academically, analyses of banking discrimination exist, the failures of specific programmes are publicly verifiable. Despite this, the configuration of instruments remains the same. The explanation lies not at the level of individual programmes but at the level of the systemic link between the donor apparatus and its interlocutors in exile, to which we turn below.

Before turning to that link, it is worth noting that the substitution of object described above is reproduced not only in donor products themselves but also in the documents intended to justify them. The academic contour: an empirical study of the Belarusian business diaspora published in early 2026 under the aegis of the FREE Network is built on a sample of 114 companies in which the IT share is 39%. This share is reproduced in the conclusions as a structural feature of the layer and used as the basis for recommendations. Meanwhile, according to the COIG register, the share of pure programming (PKD 62.01.Z) among active Belarusian companies in Poland is 5.6%, and on a broader classification including the full spectrum of IT services it is around 9%. The skew in the academic sample relative to the normal distribution is by a factor of four to seven, and it is not random: it is an artefact of respondent recruitment channels running through infrastructure historically tied to the IT community. The real-sector layer — 90–95% of the general population — gets into the sample only residually, and as a result an analysis that claims to describe the Belarusian business diaspora as a whole is in fact describing its narrow technological subset.

The expert-policy contour: the "Roadmap for a Limited De-escalation between Minsk and Brussels," published in December 2025 under the aegis of the Konrad Adenauer Foundation and prepared by a group of authors including BEROC and independent analysts, operates on the regime ↔ EU axis and proposes a step-by-step logic of sanctions easing in exchange for verifiable steps by the Belarusian side[^6]. In that discussion, the private sector as a self-standing category appears once — as the fifth of seven criteria for ranking sanctions regimes into "specific" and "sector-wide," with no internal differentiation of the real-sector segment within the private sector and no treatment of it as an object of standalone policy. A layer going through the liquidation of the mass IP status in Belarus and through banking discrimination in receiving jurisdictions appears, in a top-level expert-policy document, as a single line of a technical criterion. The FREE Network academic sample and the KAS expert-policy document are two summits of the same configuration in which the Belarusian real sector stays outside the analytical field regardless of the work's format or the composition of its authors.

### Representation gap

The second contour making the layer's invisibility stable is the gap in representation. It is customarily discussed as a problem of specific individuals — how competent or incompetent particular representatives of the Belarusian opposition in exile are in economic matters, how close to or far from real business they stand. This is important, but the discussion leads sideways. The main question is not "who exactly represents," but what configuration makes precisely this kind of representation possible and reproduces it stably over many years.

The configuration is as follows. The formal positions responsible for the economic agenda within the structures of the Belarusian opposition in exile, for much of the period 2022–2025, remained either vacant or existed in declarative form, without procedurally appointed and publicly accountable office-holders. In parallel with this formal vacancy, informal groups operated in the international space, speaking on behalf of Belarusian business at forums, in negotiations with donors, in the production of expert documents. These groups had no appointment procedure, no public accountability to Belarusian society, and no feedback mechanism with the layer they declaratively represented.

This is not a chance temporary gap. The combination of a formal vacuum and informal activity was sustained for years, and precisely during the period when the Belarusian entrepreneur was going through the liquidation of the IP status in Belarus, the doubling of registrations in Poland, the suspension of Poland Business Harbour, and growing banking discrimination in the European Union. External circumstances required precisely those decisions that neither a formal structure without responsible office-holders nor an informal group without accountability could produce. And yet the configuration was reproduced steadily, and this stable reproduction calls for explanation not at the level of individual participants' bad faith but at the level of the system in which they operate.

### The linkage

The donor frame of infantilisation and the representation gap are not two independent defects coinciding in time; they form a connected system, in which each side generates demand for the other and, at the same time, satisfies the other's demand. The donor apparatus, operating in the frame of infantilisation, needs interlocutors capable of producing precisely the content that frame requires — concepts, educational products, expert texts of a general kind, forum presentations. Representatives without an appointment procedure and without accountability to the layer match this demand perfectly. They produce the content requested in the required volumes, do not contest the frame, and do not call for its revision toward substantive work. In the opposite direction: informal representatives with access to donor resources free of public accountability have an interest in preserving precisely this donor frame, in which substantive outcomes are not required and formal reach indicators are achievable through any reproducible product.

From this mutual support follows a stable equilibrium in which both sides have an interest in preserving the status quo. If tomorrow a donor were to demand measurable changes in the position of the layer — reduced banking discrimination, increased volumes of legally relocated capital, expansion of visa programmes for verifiable entrepreneurial experience — the current configuration of representation would prove unfit. It is structurally incapable of such tasks. Symmetrically, if tomorrow the representatives were to demand from donors institutional instruments instead of trainings and guides, the existing donor infrastructure would not be ready to provide them, since it is built for a different logic of work. The 2025 CASE Working Paper "Exploring the Entrepreneurial Potential of Belarusian Migrants in the EU," commissioned by the European Commission with the Association of Belarusian Business Abroad as beneficiary, illustrates this mechanics with laboratory clarity: recommendations remain within the general frame of migrant entrepreneurship policy — simplification of administrative procedures, information centres, specialised microfinance programmes, language courses, mentoring — without designating the Belarusian real sector as a standalone object of policy and without appeal to institutional instruments specific to the Belarusian situation discussed above[^7].

Within this linkage, the layer ends up not merely outside both contours — it ends up in a position where its very invisibility is necessary for the system's stability. Turning the layer into a subject with concrete tasks and measurable needs would dismantle a configuration convenient both for donors and for their interlocutors in exile. Therefore, critique aimed at specific representatives is methodologically mistaken: it treats the symptom, leaving untouched the system that produces such representatives and reproduces them even after specific individuals are replaced. The matter is not the corruption or incompetence of particular people. The matter is a system that makes such people operationally in demand and the competent and accountable ones operationally redundant.

## Part IV. Mirror and case

### What the West ignores in its own economy

The claim that the Belarusian real-sector layer is too insignificant to warrant institutional intervention does not stand up to data on the structure of donors' own economies. According to Eurostat data for 2023–2024, the European Union has some 33.5 million enterprises, of which 99% are micro and small enterprises with up to 49 employees. These 99% account for roughly half of all employment in the EU's non-financial business economy and about a third of aggregate turnover. Large enterprises — the very ones popularly associated with the notion of "European business" — make up 0.2% of the total and account for only a third of jobs. Structurally, the backbone of the European economy is precisely the segment that the donor apparatus does not distinguish as a separate object when it turns to the Belarusian case.

This is paradoxical. In their own countries, European governments grasp the significance of micro-business clearly enough to build separate programmes of regulation, lending, and crisis support for it. During the pandemic of 2020–2021, the total volume of small- and micro-business support measures in the EU exceeded hundreds of billions of euros; in every member state there are specialised development institutions, regional funds, microfinance programmes, and tax-incentive schemes for precisely this segment.

None of the donors proposed replacing these institutions with educational guides and training; none proposed reducing the policy of supporting a Polish artisan or an Italian family-restaurant owner to "developing entrepreneurial competencies." Toward their own micro-entrepreneurs, donor countries think in infrastructural categories — banking access, tax regimes, protection against discrimination, regulatory stability.

Applied to the Belarusian real-sector layer, this logic switches off. The same micro-entrepreneur who, as a Pole in Poland, would receive support through PARP, Bank Gospodarstwa Krajowego, regional development funds, and industry associations, in the role of a "Belarusian entrepreneur in exile" receives an invitation to a webinar. The conceptual frame switches at the boundary of the category, and that switch has no empirical basis — it has its basis solely in the donor taxonomy through which the layer is seen not as a real-sector segment but as an "immigrant community in need of training."

### Poland Business Harbour: an exemplary case

The Polish programme Poland Business Harbour provides an almost laboratory-clean example of the operational consequences of applying a conceptually inadequate frame to the layer. The programme was launched in September 2020, initially to attract IT specialists and IT companies from Belarus; in 2022 it was extended to innovative-industry businesses from around the world; in January 2024 it was suspended by a decision of the Polish Ministry of Foreign Affairs with the public wording "did not live up to the hopes placed in it."

Over three and a half years of operation, more than 93,000 visas were issued. Of these, around 13,500 people actually entered Poland. About 80,000 visas were thus used by recipients not for relocation to Poland but as a means of access to the Schengen area for onward movement to other jurisdictions — Lithuania, Germany, the Netherlands, the Scandinavian countries. Formally, this is a violation of the programme's terms; substantively, it is a rational choice by recipients, because the programme was built on the premise that its typical beneficiary would want to be specifically in Poland and specifically for business, whereas the actual preferences of an IT employee with professional mobility and the logic of lawful tax-burden minimisation through jurisdiction choice turned out to be different.

The programme was built on four premises, each of which, at the moment of launch, no longer matched the structure of Belarusian business. First: the principal target beneficiary is the IT sector. As shown in the conceptual block, the share of IT in the volume of Belarusian companies in Poland is about 6%; the remaining 94% is the real sector, for which the programme provided no instruments. Second: the principal motivation of the recipient is moving to Poland to do business. The actual motivation of a substantial share of recipients had to do with mobility in the Schengen area and tax optimisation in any convenient jurisdiction, not necessarily Poland. Third: an IT employee who has opened a *jednoosobowa działalność gospodarcza* in Poland is an entrepreneur. In the classic post-Soviet scheme of "employer plus contractor on JDG," this legal form is used as a tool for the tax optimisation of wages, not as entrepreneurial activity; both the Belarusian and the Ukrainian IT markets of the 2010s worked out this model long before PBH was launched. Fourth: the Polish executive structure — the Polska Agencja Inwestycji i Handlu — is capable of verifying recipients on its own. Under limited staff resources, PAIH moved to delegating verification to a network of "trusted partners," which by the end of the programme included NGOs unrelated to IT, which inevitably led to corruption.

Each of these four errors has the same nature. The programme was designed without operational partnership with the Belarusian business communities already existing in Poland, which knew the real structure of the layer, knew the gap between declared and actual motivations of visa recipients, knew the difference between an IT freelancer and a real-sector micro-entrepreneur, and knew how post-Soviet tax optimisation through JDG works. This knowledge was available with minimal effort to obtain it. The programme was designed and implemented without this knowledge, taking by default as its object a generic "Belarusian IT entrepreneur" — a symbolic construct with little in common with the real distribution of Belarusian business by sector, size, and motivation.

This conceptual error has an observable empirical trace in the current register of Belarusian companies in Poland. As of early 2026, out of 7,093 active companies with Belarusian capital, 399 are classified under PKD 62.01.Z — programming, the basic category through which the IT sector is registered as a distinct activity. That is 5.6% of the register. By comparison, freight road transport (PKD 49.41.Z) accounts for 950 companies, that is, 2.4 times more than pure programming. Of the 13,500 people who actually entered Poland on PBH visas, a significant share are not the individual-entrepreneur startup founders the programme was built around, but employees of large corporate relocations — EPAM, Godel, Andersen, Innowise, LeverX — who moved as part of already existing structures. The actual structure of the diaspora formed during and after PBH turned out to be the opposite of the one the programme was designed for: predominantly real-sector quantitatively, predominantly corporate employment qualitatively — and neither of these two distributions corresponds to the image of the IT-entrepreneur-relocator that underlay the programme's design.

The final administrative formulation, "did not live up to the hopes placed in it," accurately describes the observable symptom — the failure of outcomes against expectations — and accurately hides the diagnosis of its cause. The programme did not live up to hopes not because its recipients turned out to be insufficiently honest, and not because the Polish side administered it too loosely. The programme did not live up to hopes because it applied to the Belarusian layer a category in which the layer structurally did not fit, and instruments that answered a non-existent request. This is not an operational error; it is a conceptual one.

### Generalisation

PBH is not a Polish error. It is a local manifestation of the same conceptual gap described in Part III. In any EU country where there is no addressable category of "Belarusian real-sector entrepreneur" and where donor and state thinking oscillates between the two ill-fitting frames — tech startup and nascent entrepreneur — support programmes will be built either for the first or for the second, and in both cases they will miss 94% of the layer's actual volume.

Notably, this regularity works the same way in very different institutional settings. Polish PBH failed because it took the IT sector as its object. Lithuanian, Latvian, and Estonian programmes for supporting Belarusian business in exile, in one form or another, take as their object the "immigrant startup founder" with a basic need for entrepreneurship training. Brussels programmes of the European Commission directed at the Belarusian private sector operate in the logic of a standardised donor product for developing countries. Different addressees, different institutional environments, different political contexts — and the same conceptual error: applying to a layer a category it does not fit, with the result that work is done with the layer's symbolic stand-in, not with the layer itself.

## Part V. Reframing

### What has to change at the level of taxonomy

The principal change, without which all others will remain cosmetic, lies not at the level of programmes and not at the level of institutions, but at the level of categories through which the Belarusian real-sector layer is seen by the donor apparatus. So long as only two frames are available in this taxonomy — tech startup and nascent entrepreneur — any support programme will be built around one of them and will miss the real object.

Introducing into the donor lexicon an addressable category describing a real-sector entrepreneur who has lost the institutional environment of their own country and needs not training but reintegration into a new jurisdiction is not a rhetorical gesture but the first operational step, after which everything else becomes possible.

This category must be distinguishable from existing ones along two axes. Along the first axis, it differs from the tech startup in that it assumes no arbitrage mobility and no high added value from innovation; its bearer is a materially anchored real-sector micro-entrepreneur for whom the value is not venture growth but sustainable restoration of operational activity in a new jurisdiction. Along the second axis, it differs from the nascent entrepreneur in that it assumes no initial training and implies no deficit of entrepreneurial competencies; its bearer arrives with decades of operational experience and needs institutional support for the conversion of that experience into a new legal and financial environment, not the reproduction of entry-level trainings. Only the introduction of this category as a category of its own — not as a sub-type of one of the two existing ones — makes possible the appearance of instruments matching the layer's real tasks.

### What has to change at the level of instruments

From the categorical reset follows a revision of the entire instrumental range. The educational product ceases to be the principal format of support, because it does not answer the request that actually exists in the layer. In its place comes infrastructural work in three directions, each of which is technical rather than educational in nature.

The first direction is mobility and the right to economic activity. Visa programmes built around the new object must rest on verifiable entrepreneurial experience in Belarus rather than on formal affiliation with the technology sector; the tools for such verification already exist in the form of tax and registration statistics on Belarusian individual entrepreneurship for previous periods. In parallel, the issue of Belarusian access to individual entrepreneurship in Poland (*jednoosobowa działalność gospodarcza*, the analogue of the Belarusian IP[^3]) requires a systemic solution: under current legislation, it is open only to holders of certain legal statuses such as the *Karta Stałego Pobytu* or the *Karta Polaka*, while for Belarusians on ordinary migration statuses this legal form is unavailable. Targeted exceptions were made for Ukrainians after February 2022 and for PBH visa holders, but precisely in the logic of exceptions, not a systemic regime. The scale of those exceptions is telling: for Ukrainians, a special regime tied to the PESEL UKR status opened direct access to JDG without the *Karta Pobytu*, and through this channel new individual-entrepreneurship registrations by Ukrainian citizens in 2024 numbered in the tens of thousands per year. For Belarusians, no analogous regime has been introduced, although the structural need is comparable and, in terms of access to capital and banking services, more acute. A declarative policy of supporting Belarusian business coexists with a legal regime that structurally fails to admit this layer's basic entrepreneurial activity.

The second direction is capital. This includes both the legalisation of capital being moved out of Belarus through the adaptation of existing mechanisms of tax repatriation in receiving jurisdictions, and access to starting capital in the receiving country through specialised credit lines and state guarantees for Belarusian micro-enterprises with a verified history of activity. None of these solutions requires development from scratch; they require extending existing instruments to a new object.

The third direction is non-discrimination. Transparent compliance protocols for Belarusian legal entities with residency in the receiving jurisdiction, worked out jointly with the banking sector and regulators, move the conversation from the plane of "banks have the right to choose their clients" to the plane of a technical standard applied predictably. This relieves the entrepreneur of the need to prove their reliability anew at every contact with the financial system and at the same time gives the banking sector clear criteria for working with a category that is currently cut off pre-emptively.

Notably, in all three directions the receiving state — Poland first of all — already has most of the necessary infrastructure. The donors' task is not to build parallel assets of their own but to finance the missing links and provide political backing for the adaptation of existing instruments to the new object.

### What has to change at the level of partnership

The third level of reframing concerns who interacts with whom in the design and delivery of support. The current configuration, described in Part III, is built on the scheme "donors → nominal opposition representatives in exile → business," in which the middle link produces content convenient to the donor frame but is bound by no reciprocal obligations to the layer for whose sake the work is being done. This scheme is reproduced steadily and will not unlock itself.

An alternative configuration — "donors plus the receiving state plus active immigrant structures supporting Belarusian business → business" — assumes that the middle link is formed not of representatives with a declarative mandate but of operational partners with a verifiable connection to the layer and accountability to it through daily work with its real participants. Such structures exist in Poland and Lithuania, including in the form of industry associations and immigrant business communities; the question is not their creation but their recognition by the donor apparatus as legitimate partners instead of, or alongside, the structures currently occupying that place[^4]. The defining feature of such a configuration is direct operational contact with the self-employed and micro-entrepreneurs, not mediated by representative structures.

From this reset follows a change in the language of the conversation. Instead of negotiations about "support for the Belarusian private sector in exile" in the generic sense, there appears the possibility of negotiations about measurable tasks: about visa protocols, about banking standards, about mechanisms for the legalisation of capital, about specialised credit lines. Each of these tasks has its responsible parties, its deadlines, and KPIs of outcome rather than KPIs of reach. Each of them requires operational expertise, not the ability to produce expert text of a general kind. And each of them simultaneously makes impossible the configuration that exists now, since the latter is structurally incapable of work of this type.

## Conclusion

The thesis that the Belarusian private sector is "the last hope of sovereignty and transformation" was formulated by independent academic literature in 2023. Over the nearly three years since, it has been reproduced many times — in studies, in public statements, in donor strategy documents, in the rhetoric of Belarusian political structures in exile. At the level of declarations, it is treated as part of the consensus.

At the level of operational reality, during the same three years, the following has happened. The Belarusian real-sector layer in Belarus has lost approximately fifty thousand active individual entrepreneurs through forced administrative dismantling. The Polish Poland Business Harbour programme, the only significant visa initiative targeted at Belarusian business, was suspended with the wording "did not live up to expectations." Banking discrimination on national grounds has acquired a steady character. Access to the basic legal form of individual entrepreneurship in the main receiving jurisdiction remains closed to most Belarusians. The structural reorientation of the in-country part of the layer toward servicing the state sector and the Russian market, described in the second part, has passed the point beyond which its reversibility in the foreseeable future requires not additional efforts but several times the effort that would have been needed in case of timely intervention. The only Western expert structure that consistently worked with the Belarusian SME sector as a standalone object before 2022 — the German Economic Team — suspended its advisory activity on Belarus after February 2022 and over the nearly four years since has replaced it with neither a programme aimed at the relocated layer nor a programme aimed at the one remaining inside the country[^8]. In parallel, the Belarusian business case has dropped out of the active agenda of most Western think tanks that previously worked on the region: even those centres that keep the topic in their publication plans look at it through macroeconomic and sanctions lenses, not through the lens of the real-sector layer as a standalone object. The position responsible for the economic agenda in the nominal opposition has remained formally vacant for almost all of this time.

The gap between the declarative significance of the layer and the reality of its situation is the very phenomenon this essay seeks to describe. The work has proposed reading that gap not as a sum of separate failures, where one ought to repeat the same intervention and expect a result, but as a stable configuration in which the invisibility of the Belarusian entrepreneur is an operational condition of the entire system's functioning — of the donor apparatus, of its interlocutors in exile, and of the lens through which the Western observer sees the Belarusian case. This configuration is reproduced not out of ill will and not out of the individual bad faith of its participants; it is reproduced through the logic of mutual convenience that each of the sides extracts from the existing order of things.

From this follows also the character of a possible exit. Additional funding for current programmes will reproduce the same configuration on a larger scale. Replacing specific representatives in exile with more competent or more honest ones will reproduce the same configuration with new faces. Launching new programmes within the existing donor frame will reproduce the same configuration under new names. Change is possible only at the level of the categorical frame through which the Belarusian real-sector layer first becomes visible as a standalone object of policy with its own measurable tasks, its own responsible implementers, and its own horizon of outcomes.

The fundamental difference between diagnosis and programme is that a programme is an instrument designed inside an existing frame; a diagnosis is an argument for revising the frame itself. The essay offers the second, not the first. Without revising the frame, no programme makes sense, because it will be an instrument of the frame's reproduction; once the frame is revised, the programme becomes a technical task with a multitude of possible solutions. The time during which this fork still makes sense is bounded not by the political calendar and not by the duration of sanctions, but by the speed with which the layer's daily operational ties are being restructured in a direction from which a return is structurally impossible.

[^1]: Resolution of the Council of Ministers of the Republic of Belarus No. 457 of 28 June 2024 "On the Types of Individual Entrepreneurial Activity" and Law No. 365-Z of 22 April 2024 "On Amendments to the Laws on Entrepreneurial Activity."

[^2]: See, in particular, the FREE Policy Brief 2023 "Belarusian Business in Turbulent Times" and the FREE Network publications of 2023–2025 on the structure of the Belarusian private sector and the entrepreneurial diaspora.

[^3]: *Jednoosobowa działalność gospodarcza* (JDG) is the Polish legal form of individual entrepreneurship, functionally analogous to the Belarusian or Russian IP.

[^4]: The author participated in developing the concept of the MOST project in 2023; this essay uses materials from that work to illustrate a logic not admitted by the current donor configuration.

[^5]: Astapenia, R. "Belarus's Economic Realignment: From Western Markets to Russian Dependency," Chatham House Research Paper, January 2026; the article draws, in particular, on the empirical estimates in Kruk, D. "Belarusian Economy and Russian Sanctions," FREE Policy Brief, FREE Network, October 2024.

[^6]: Bushilo, H., Deikalo, A., Kruk, D., Shraibman, A. "A Roadmap for a Limited De-escalation between Minsk and Brussels," Konrad-Adenauer-Stiftung, December 2025.

[^7]: Aleszko-Lessels, O., Kulesa, A., Zarychta, M. "Exploring the Entrepreneurial Potential of Belarusian Migrants in the EU," CASE Working Paper No. 143/2025, Center for Social and Economic Research, Warsaw, 2025; prepared within the European Commission-funded project "Support to Belarusian Businesses in Exile and Association of Belarusian Business Abroad (ABBA) Capacity Development."

[^8]: The German Economic Team is an advisory programme funded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action and implemented by Berlin Economics, which provided analytical support to the government and regulators of Belarus from 2003 to 2022. According to a public statement on the programme's Belarus page, "advisory activities in Belarus are suspended for the time being." Over 2022–2025, the programme has not launched an alternative track of work either with the relocated Belarusian SME sector or with the one remaining inside the country.
